persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: 8 officials share the surname Conroy, and the gap between the bio's appointment date (1880) and the stint's first listing (1889) could in theory conceal a succession — a different J. G. Conroy taking over the bench. This fails: the office is a specific senior judicial post ('Central District Court judge') matched verbatim in the same colony, both initials agree exactly, and the bio and stint co-occur in the same editions across more than two decades (1889 through 1915) with colony-agreeing position matches — the biographical entry naming this exact judgeship was reprinted in the very volumes whose Newfoundland staff lists carry J. G. Conroy as Central District Court Judge. A succession by a same-initialled relative while the original's biography continued to be printed unchanged for 25+ years is not a realistic hypothesis; the stint's start at 1889 simply reflects coverage, not appointment.

evidence_cited.fact: J. G. Conroy | Central District Court Judge | Judicial Establishment

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of two J. G. Conroys on the Newfoundland bench (e.g. an obituary of the 1880 appointee before 1915 while the staff listing continued), which would indicate a same-name successor.

notes: Multi-edition co-occurrence check passes (2+ agreeing edition-years at pos~99); position, colony, and both initials agree exactly over a 27-year span. The 1898 department string 'NEWFOUNDLAND—NEW SOUTH WALES' is a page-header OCR artifact, not a posting change.
